title: Egg Cookery
content:
SOFT-COOKED

Place eggs in boiling water; reduce heat to 185°F. Hold at temperature: 4 minutes for soft-cooked (white tender, yolk thick cream). Remove; serve immediately or plunge into ice water to stop cooking.

HARD-COOKED

Same method as soft-cooked but hold at 185°F for 45 minutes. Result: firm, tender white; yolk thoroughly cooked, mealy when broken. Do not use eggs fresher than 3 days old (difficult to peel; may turn greenish). Cool in ice water 5 minutes or in 32°F air 20 minutes.

Test for hard-cooked: Spin on pointed end—hard-cooked spins like top; raw topples over.

POACHED

Break egg; slip white and yolk into liquid at 185°F (water, broth, milk, or vegetable liquor). Hold until white firm and yolk covered with thin white film. Do not boil.

BAKED

Combine with soft bread crumbs; place in buttered ramekins; bake moderate oven (375°F).

SCRAMBLED/OMELETS

Cook at moderate temperatures to prevent toughening. Stir constantly for creamy texture. Eggs should be 65-75°F at outset for best volume and texture.

MERINGUES

Beat whites to stiff but not dry. For more stable foam: add 1 teaspoon sugar per egg white when foamy (reduces volume slightly but gives better structure). Bake slow oven (325°F) about 20 minutes until tender, evenly browned, and firm enough to hold shape. Watery meringue = underbaked; tough/shriveled = too hot.
